fed_monetary: The Fed holds the funds rate at 3.50–3.75% for the second consecutive meeting, with Chair Powell explicitly citing Iran-war-driven energy inflation and sticky producer prices as barriers to easing. Two dissenters—Governors Miran and Waller—preferred a 25 bps cut, while market-based measures now price only one cut in 2026, down from two earlier in the year. The balance sheet runs at $6.694T (WALCL), RRP has collapsed to $0.3B, and reserve balances of $3.116T remain ample, leaving financial conditions modestly accommodative (NFCI -0.465) despite the policy pause.
labor_data: The March labor market delivered a clean beat: NFP printed +178K against a +100K consensus, while the unemployment rate held at 4.3% in line with estimates—a resilient but decelerating picture consistent with a soft landing rather than re-acceleration. Initial claims of 219K (vs. 210K est.) introduce a mild caution signal at the margin, suggesting early-cycle softening in hiring demand may be emerging. With wages still elevated and labor supply pressures from geopolitical disruptions, the Fed's dual-mandate tension remains skewed toward inflation over employment risk.
fiscal_supply: The March 2026 federal deficit registered approximately $249 billion—roughly 2% wider than the same month in 2025—sustaining elevated net Treasury issuance that pressures the term premium. Treasury's quarterly refunding offered $125B to refund ~$90.2B of maturing securities, and 4-week bill issuance has surged to a $101B-per-auction average in 2026 (vs. $47B prior), reflecting front-end financing dependence. The TGA balance of $748.4B provides a near-term liquidity buffer, but the structural deficit trajectory and above-trend coupon supply keep the 10Y anchored at 4.30%, suppressing duration appetite.
corporate: Q1 2026 earnings season opens with S&P 500 EPS growth consensus at +12.5% YoY, led by Financials (+7% for Diversified Banks) and AI-linked Technology; the SPX trades at 6,992, up 0.36% on the session with VIX at 17.99—complacency territory absent a clear macro shock. HY OAS of 284 bps and IG OAS of 102 bps reflect spread compression to tight-but-not-extreme levels; all-in yields remain healthy and fundamentals do not point to imminent credit stress. However, BofA projects headline PCE re-accelerating to ~3.1% and core to ~3.5% in March as tariff pass-through materializes, which poses a margin-compression risk for cost-sensitive sectors in H2.
next_catalyst: March Advance Retail Sales (rescheduled to April 21) is the single highest-priority release: the Chicago Fed's CARTS model projects ex-auto sales +0.9% MoM nominally but -1.1% in real terms, and a soft print would sharpen the stagflation narrative—hot PPI at 6.03% alongside weakening consumer demand—forcing markets to reassess the earnings growth trajectory and the timing of any Fed pivot.
sources: Fed holds rates at 3.50–3.75%, Powell flags Iran war inflation risk, Miran and Waller dissent for cut — Federal Reserve / PBS NewsHour
March PPI +6.03% YoY vs. 2.6% est.; BofA sees March core PCE re-accelerating to ~3.5%, 'keeping the Fed firmly on hold' — BLS / BofA via FinancialContent
Q1 2026 S&P 500 earnings growth seen at +12.5% YoY; Financials sector banks project +7% growth — FactSet / S&P Global
March 2026 federal deficit ~$249B, ~2% wider YoY; Treasury 4-week bill issuance surges to $101B per auction average — CBO / Bipartisan Policy Center
March Advance Retail Sales rescheduled to April 21; Chicago Fed CARTS projects ex-auto sales +0.9% nominal, -1.1% real — U.S. Census Bureau / Chicago Fed
